beats.prompt: Warm up: match each idea to its answer.

pairs.left: Solve a sequential game from the end

feedback.correct: Right. Today: in take-away games every position is either winning or losing — and a single rule (XOR or mod) decides which.
hints: Unravel a sequential game from the last move backward: backward induction.
A position where every available move hands the opponent a winning spot — the mover loses: P-position.
With 5 pirates and 100 coins, backward induction gives the senior pirate 98.

beats.prompt: Take 1–3 tokens, last to take WINS. Pile of 12. The first player…

wins.note: Not quite. 12 is a multiple of 4 — that makes it a P-position (Previous player wins). No matter how many tokens the first player takes, the second can always return the pile to the next multiple of 4.

loses.note: Yes. 12 = 4 × 3 is a P-position. Whatever the first player takes (1, 2, or 3), the second player takes 4 minus that to reach 8, then 4, then 0. The first player loses with perfect play.

depends.note: No opponent-choice dependence here. With 12 tokens both players play optimally: 12 is a multiple of 4 so the first player is in a losing position — no sequence of play can change that.

beats.prompt: A quick definition before the board.

interaction.title: P-positions & N-positions
interaction.body: Every position in a take-away game is either a P-position (Previous player wins — the mover LOSES) or an N-position (Next player wins — the mover WINS). Terminal position (0 tokens): P-position (the mover has lost). From an N-position there is always at least one move to a P-position. From a P-position every move leads to an N-position. Winning strategy: always hand your opponent a P-position. In the subtraction game (remove 1 to k tokens), the P-positions are exactly the multiples of k+1.

feedback.correct: Keep that in mind: you win by always handing your opponent a P-position.
hints: P = Previous player wins (mover loses); N = Next player wins (mover wins).
From a P-position every move gives the opponent an N-position — there is no escape.
Read and tap Continue.

beats.prompt: Take 1–3 tokens; last to take wins. Pile of 10. How many should you take?

feedback.correct: Take 2, leaving 8 — a multiple of 4. Whatever your opponent takes (1, 2, or 3), respond with 4 minus that: land on 4, then 0. You win.
hints: P-positions are multiples of 4: 0, 4, 8, 12, …
10 mod 4 = 2. Taking 2 leaves 8, a P-position for your opponent.
Winning move: take 2 → leave 8.

beats.prompt: Same rules, smaller pile: 7 tokens, take 1–3. How many should you take?

feedback.correct: Take 3, leaving 4 — a P-position. Then mirror your opponent to reach 0.
hints: P-positions are multiples of 4: 0, 4, 8, …
7 mod 4 = 3. Take 3 to leave 4.
Winning move: take 3 → leave 4.

beats.prompt: Three heaps: 3, 4, and 5 tokens. Last to take wins. The nim-sum (XOR of all heaps) decides who wins — explore the board.

feedback.correct: Nim-sum 3⊕4⊕5 = 2 ≠ 0, so the first player wins. Winning move: reduce the 3-heap to 1, leaving (1, 4, 5) with nim-sum 1⊕4⊕5 = 0 — a P-position your opponent cannot escape.
hints: Compute 3 XOR 4 = 7, then 7 XOR 5 = 2. Non-zero nim-sum ⇒ first player wins.
Find a heap h where h XOR 2 < h. The 3-heap: 3 XOR 2 = 1 < 3 — reduce it to 1.
Winning move: reduce the 3-heap to 1 → leave (1, 4, 5), nim-sum 0.

beats.prompt: Three lenses on Nim. Predict the core rule, then reveal each frame.

lenses.label: nim-sum = 0 ⟺ losing
lenses.body: A position is a P-position (mover loses) if and only if its nim-sum — the XOR of all heap sizes — equals 0. Any move from a nim-sum-0 position changes at least one heap, making the XOR non-zero and handing the opponent an N-position.

lenses.label: XOR balances the heaps
lenses.body: From any N-position (nim-sum ≠ 0), you can always find a move that zeroes the XOR: pick any heap whose leading bit matches the nim-sum's leading bit and reduce it until the XOR cancels. Your opponent is then stuck at nim-sum 0.

lenses.label: Mirror onto a P-position
lenses.body: Winning play is a strategy of keeping nim-sum 0 after every move you make. You hand the opponent a P-position each turn; they are forced to produce an N-position, which you correct again — until they take the last token and lose.

feedback.correct: nim-sum 0 ⇒ P-position (the mover loses). The XOR rule is exact: every non-zero nim-sum has a zeroing move; every zero nim-sum has none.
hints: nim-sum = XOR of all heap sizes. Zero ⇒ P-position (mover loses).
From a non-zero nim-sum you can always move to zero. From zero, any move makes it non-zero.
Three lenses: algebraic (XOR = 0), strategic (balancing), and positional (mirroring).

beats.interviewNote: Green Book chocolate-bar invariant: each break splits one piece into two, so the piece count increases by exactly 1 per break. Starting from 1 piece you need exactly mn − 1 breaks to reach mn unit squares — the number is forced, not strategic. Coins on a round table (techinterview.org symmetry / strategy-stealing): the first player places a coin at the exact center, then mirrors every opponent move through the center — guaranteeing the first player always has a legal move when the opponent does, so the first player wins. Normal vs misère Nim: in normal play (last to take wins) the nim-sum rule applies exactly; in misère Nim (last to take loses) play normally until only heaps of size 0 or 1 remain, then take an odd number of those last tokens instead of even. The 21 game (take 1–3, the player forced to take 21 loses) is misère subtraction — P-positions shift by one.
beats.prompt: A chocolate bar is 6 × 8 = 48 squares. You snap it along grid lines until you have 48 individual unit squares. What is the minimum number of breaks required?

feedback.correct: 47. Each break takes one piece and produces two — exactly +1 piece per break. You start with 1 piece and need 48, so you need exactly 47 breaks. No strategy can speed this up: it is forced (mn − 1 = 6 × 8 − 1 = 47).
hints: After k breaks, how many pieces exist? Each break adds exactly one.
Start with 1 piece; after 47 breaks you have 48 pieces.

beats.prompt: Nim heaps (1, 4, 5). Compute the nim-sum and decide who wins.

interaction.scenario: Three Nim heaps: sizes 1, 4, and 5. The nim-sum is \(1 \oplus 4 \oplus 5\), where \(\oplus\) denotes bitwise XOR. Recall: a position with nim-sum \(0\) is a P-position — the player to move loses with perfect play.

fields.label: Does the player to move win? (yes/no)

feedback.correct: \(1 \oplus 4 \oplus 5 = 5 \oplus 5 = 0\). Nim-sum 0 ⇒ P-position: the player to move loses. Despite the large 5-heap, every move produces a non-zero XOR the opponent can correct. \[ 1 \oplus 4 \oplus 5 = 0. \]
hints: 1 in binary is 001; 4 is 100; 5 is 101. XOR column by column: 001 XOR 100 = 101 = 5.
Then 5 XOR 5 = 0. nim-sum = 0.
nim-sum = 0 ⇒ P-position ⇒ the mover loses → answer: no.

feedback.correct: Every impartial take-away position is winning (N) or losing (P): you win by always handing your opponent a P-position. In Nim the nim-sum (XOR) = 0 ⟺ P-position. In subtraction (remove 1–k), P-positions are the multiples of k+1. Some 'games' are forced — no strategy, just counting. You've now seen the full Game Theory arc: dominance → Nash equilibrium → mixed strategies → minimax → backward induction → winning positions.
hints: nim-sum = XOR of all heaps; 0 ⇒ P-position (mover loses).
Subtraction game: P-positions are multiples of k+1; winning move is n mod (k+1).
Arc: dominance (L1) → Nash (L2) → mixing (L3) → minimax (L4) → backward induction (L5) → winning positions (L6).
